title: Hot dog buns
subtitle: Soft, split American-style rolls
excerpt: Soft white rolls with a top split, lightly enriched with milk and a little butter. Split horizontally from the top to hold a sausage without the bun rolling away.
sourceNotes: American soft roll tradition, documented in early-twentieth-century US domestic cookery. The top-split design was standardised by commercial bakeries in the 1940s to allow the rolls to be baked and separated cleanly. UK home-baking version follows the same enriched dough principle as the dinner roll.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled in a bag for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before and store in a bag at room temperature. The soft crumb stays fresh for 2 days.

content.text: Hot dog buns bake in a touching row so they split cleanly apart at the table. The top split is made after baking with a sharp knife — or the rolls can be part-split before baking so the top crust opens during the oven spring. The dough is lightly enriched rather than properly brioche: enough to give a soft, white crumb without the richness that would compete with the filling.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Mix {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{salt-table}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. Add the {{whole-milk}}, {{water}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} and bring together. Knead for 8–10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and leave until doubled, about 1 hour.

Divide into 8 equal pieces. Shape each into a sausage about 12 cm long, tapering the ends slightly. Arrange in a row on a lined tray, touching, with the long sides together. Cover and prove for 40 minutes until puffed. Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan).

Brush with milk or beaten egg. Bake for 14–16 minutes until light golden. The undersides should be pale gold, not deep brown — these are soft buns, not crusty rolls. Cool on a rack. Once cool, split each bun down the top with a serrated knife, cutting three-quarters of the way through.
